data.title: SPME: An 7.875% Term Preferred IPO From Sound Point Meridian Capital
data.description:
Co-authored by Relative Value.

Overview

Our primary investment interest lies in the fixed-income segment of the market, so we need to keep ourselves up to date with every new IPO in this small universe. Conducting a thorough analysis of each newly listed preferred stock and baby bond, as well as the issuer, is our method for determining whether this new security can be a valuable addition to our portfolio. This informative IPO article will be dedicated to the Sound Point Meridian Capital's (NYSE:SPMC) latest addition to the exchange - the 7.875% Series B Preferred Shares (SPME). SPMC is a relatively new closed-end investment company, having gone through its IPO a year ago. Luckily for us, the company issued the same type of security as SPME at the beginning of 2025, which we have covered, so we have a reliable comparison basis to step on when conducting the current IPO analysis.

The New Issue

All the relevant information regarding the new preferred issue can be found in its prospectus. Here we will point out only the most important metrics for us, which we will use in the analysis that follows.

SPME pays a 7.875% annual cumulative dividend in $0.1641 monthly distributions. Sound Point Meridian Capital's gross proceeds of the IPO are $57.5 million for a total of 2.3 million preferred shares issued with a $25 par value each. The new preferreds did not receive a credit rating at IPO by any of the big three rating agencies. The preferred stock has a maturity date of 07/31/2030 and is callable on and after 07/31/2027.

As the issue is just listed, its price chart looks like this:

Currently, SPME is priced at $24.99 with a Yield to Maturity of 8.18% and the Yield to Call is calculated to be 8.21%. This is what the stock's YTC curve looks like right now:

The Company

Founded in 2022 and headquartered in New York, Sound Point Meridian Capital went public in 2024. It is a non-diversified, closed-end management investment company, externally managed by Sound Point Meridian Management Company, LLC. The stated main investment objective of the company is to generate high income, with capital appreciation set as a secondary goal. The means of achieving these investment objectives is by constructing a portfolio, consisting predominantly of equity and mezzanine tranches of USD-denominated collateralized loan obligations. Junior debt tranches of CLOs and loan accumulation facilities find smaller exposure in the company's portfolio. This is what the company's portfolio breakdown by different criteria looks like, as of the end of May 2025:

The asset type breakdown shows that almost 98% of the company's portfolio is invested in CLO-Equity tranches. These represent the positions that take the first loss if things go bad, but also are entitled to all the surplus interest and principal collections from the underlying portfolio, if any.

SPMC's Performance

The daily chart below shows how the market evaluates the common stock of SPMC since its IPO:

For one year since its listing on the exchange, the company was priced with an average premium of 1.74%, currently at a 3.62% discount, as per the last reported NAV per share of $18.50 at the end of June 2025:

SPMC does not have a long history of being listed, but for the one year on the exchange, it has outperformed in the total return metric other, better-known companies that hold portfolios with heavy CLO-equity exposure:

Dividends

Above is shown the dividend history of SPMC since its IPO a year ago. The distributions are monthly, and if we annualize the past one, we can calculate a 16.82% forward annual yield. The company has 20.62 million common shares outstanding. An annual distribution of $3 per share adds up to a total of $61.9 million in common shares dividends for the following twelve months. The previously issued term preferred stock of the company SPMA has an annual distribution of $4 million, and SPME of $4.5 million. The common shares' dividend provides a sufficient cushion for the preferred distributions, covering them more than twice.

Asset Coverage Ratio

The mandatory requirement under the Investment Company Act of 1940 for a regulated company is to maintain at least 200% asset coverage on its preferred shares outstanding. The numerator in this formula is the total assets of the company less the liabilities, which are not senior securities, or $437.3 million. The denominator is the senior securities of the company, or in the particular case, the $57.5 million face value outstanding of SPMA, plus the same face value for the new issue SPME, for a total of $115 million. The so calculated asset coverage ratio, as per the last reported book values, and including the new term preferred stock, is 380%, or well above the mandatory requirements.

IPO comparison

Sound Point Meridian Capital has already listed a five-year term preferred stock, SPMA, half a year ago, so we have a solid comparison basis. In the evaluation of the new issue, we'll use the latest listed term securities - preferred stocks and baby bonds of the other CLO-equity holding companies on the exchange - Oxford Lane Capital Corporation (OXLC), OFS Credit Company Inc (OCCI), Eagle Point Credit Co LLC (ECC), and Pearl Diver Credit Company (PDCC):

The average value of 4.02% IPO credit spread is for the five securities besides SPME. The risk premium for the new term preferred is in line with the average of the latest issued securities in the sector. Particular interest for us is the comparison to the IPO credit spread of SPMA, showing that, according to the underwriters, there is no worsening of the credit quality of the issuer SPMC for the past half a year.

Sector Comparison

The charts below contain all baby bonds and term preferreds with maturity dates that pay a fixed interest rate in the Closed-End Fund — Debt sector. As SPMЕ is trading around par value, we can compare it once to all the securities in the set by yield to maturity and a second time to those trading before their call date and priced above par by yield to call:

The same securities with all the relevant metrics:

Both comparisons show that the new term preferred SPME is adequately priced compared to its direct peers, issued by companies holding portfolios with collateralized loan obligations. Although not having the best YTM in the set, SPME is pretty close to it and presents a nice diversification opportunity for investors interested in the sector.

Use of Proceeds

We intend to use the proceeds from the sale of shares of Series B Preferred Shares pursuant to this prospectus to acquire investments in accordance with our investment objectives and strategies described in this prospectus and for general working capital purposes. Additionally, we may use the proceeds from this offering to pay down debt acquired under our CIBC Credit Facility, which facility may be used for working capital and for general corporate purposes. Under the terms of the CIBC Credit Facility, we initially can borrow up to $100 million, subject to certain restrictions, at an interest rate of SOFR plus a margin of 3.75% per annum at the time of drawdown, but we can convert our borrowings to the alternate base rate plus a margin of 2.75% per annum under certain conditions. The CIBC Credit Facility matures on July 8, 2026. We cannot estimate the approximate amount intended to be used for each of these purposes. Such amounts will depend on our cashflow needs after the closing of the offering, market conditions, and other factors. We currently anticipate that it will take approximately three to six months to invest substantially all of the net proceeds of this offering intended for our targeted investments, depending on the availability of appropriate investment opportunities consistent with our investment objectives and market conditions.

Source: SPME Prospectus

Special clauses in the prospectus

If we fail to maintain asset coverage (as defined in Section 18(h) of the 1940 Act) of at least 200% as of the close of business on the last business day of any calendar quarter and such failure is not cured by the close of business on the date that is 30 calendar days following the filing date of our Annual Report on Form N-CSR, Semiannual Report on Form N-CSRS or Reports on Form N-PORT, as applicable, for that quarter, or the “Asset Coverage Cure Date,” then we will be required to redeem, within 90 calendar days of the Asset Coverage Cure Date, the number of shares of our preferred stock (which at our discretion may include any number or portion of the Series B Preferred Shares), that, when combined with any debt securities redeemed for failure to maintain the asset coverage required by the indenture governing such securities (if applicable), (1) results in us having asset coverage of at least 200%, or (2) if fewer, the maximum number of shares of our preferred stock that can be redeemed out of funds legally available for such redemption. In connection with any redemption for failure to maintain such asset coverage, we may, in our sole option, redeem such additional number of shares of our preferred stock that will result in asset coverage up to and including 285%.

Source: SPME Prospectus

Conclusion

This is an informational article about the new term preferred stock SPME. With these articles, we want to pay attention to all new preferred stocks and baby bonds, and they are a good guide to what to expect from your income portfolio.

Sound Point Meridian Capital's new term preferred stock, SPME, has an IPO credit spread in line with the last issues of the companies holding portfolios of CLO-equity. The company shows no deterioration of its credit quality, compared with half a year ago. For the short time being listed on the exchange, the company outperforms its direct peers in total return. The asset coverage ratio for its term preferreds is close to twice the mandatory requirement by law. The asset coverage covenants give another layer of safety to the preferred. Priced close to par, SPME presents a compelling investment opportunity and a possible diversification opportunity for investors. On a selloff below par, I'd like it even better. I give it a "Buy" crore.

data.title: Regions Financial: Use The Preferred Shares As An Income Boost
data.description:
Introduction

Regions Financial (NYSE:RF) is focusing on Texas, the Midwest, and the Southern part of the United States, and with a balance sheet size of almost $160B, it has become one of the larger regional banking groups in the US. The bank’s financial results remain solid, and this makes a position in both the common shares and the preferred shares potentially interesting.

A robust financial result, with fewer non-performing loans

Looking at the bank’s Q2 income statement, we see Regions Financial reported a total interest income of $1.78B, an increase of almost $60M or in excess of 3% compared to the Q1 interest income. Meanwhile, the total interest expense decreased by approximately 1%, resulting in a 5% increase in the net interest income.

As the income statement below shows, the bank’s non-interest income increased to $646M on the back of a lower loss on the sale of securities, while its non-interest expenses also increased due to higher staff expenses.

This ultimately resulted in a pre-tax and pre-provision income of approximately $822M, an increase compared to the $745M in Q1 2025. On an after-tax basis, the net profit was $563M, of which $534M was attributable to the common shareholders of Regions Financial. This works out to approximately $0.59 per share, and this brings the H1 EPS to $1.11 per share based on the weighted average share count (the current share count is approximately 1% lower than the weighted average share count during the semester).

Meanwhile, as you can see in the image above, the ratio of non-performing loans as a percentage of the total loan book has decreased on a QoQ basis as well as on a YoY basis, and that’s an encouraging sign. While I don’t want to read too much into a quarterly comparison and the full impact of tariffs on consumer confidence levels still has to hit the USA in the second semester, the robust asset quality allows Regions Financial to keep its quarterly loan loss provisions limited. On its Q2 conference call, Regions Financial’s management also pointed out that it saw some improving underlying credit metrics during the quarter. It also reconfirmed its ‘through-the-cycle’ net charge-offs of 40-50 bps per year.

The preferred shares remain attractive

Last year, Regions Financial issued a new series of preferred shares, the Series F preferred shares, which are trading with (RF.PR.F) as a ticker symbol. Just like most other preferred securities issued by financial institutions, these preferred shares are non-cumulative, and this specific issue offers a fixed preferred dividend yield of 6.95% in the first five years. The preferred dividend will be payable in four equal quarterly tranches of just over $0.434 per share. From September 2029 on, this series of preferred shares will see the preferred dividend yield being reset for a subsequent period of five years.

Unlike most other fixed-to-float preferreds, the new preferred dividend isn’t based on the three-month SOFR, as the quarterly resettable preferred shares tend to be based on, but Regions Financial will be using the five-year US Treasury rate plus a markup of 277.1 bps. The 5-Year US Treasury Note is currently yielding 3.96%, so if the reset happened today, it would reset to a 6.73% preferred dividend yield based on the $25 principal value of the security. Interestingly, according to Chatham Financial, the forward curve for the 5-Year US Treasury yield indicates an upward pattern.

Of course, a lot can still happen between now and 2029, and I’m not even taking an 80 bps increase in the 5Y US Treasury yield into consideration, but right now, it doesn’t look like having a 5Y resettable yield is a negative issue.

That being said, this also increases the call risk of this series of preferred shares. Although a markup of 277 bps to the 5Y US Treasury is very reasonable, Regions Financial could decide to call this security, which would result in a 1% capital loss given the current share price of $25.25. But again, this is all theoretical and hypothetical in nature, and we’ll have to wait until 2028 at the earliest to have a better idea of the potential reset rates and the call risk.

Investment thesis

As I’m based in the Eurozone, I have been slowly increasing my exposure to USD investments given the continuous weakness in the US Dollar. Receiving almost 7% from a (non-cumulative) preferred stock issued by a sizeable bank in the USA is quite appealing, and the 5-year reset ensures good visibility on future preferred dividends.

Meanwhile, the common shares are trading at around 11 times earnings, and with a dividend yield of just around 4.05% based on the current $26 share price and the announced increase, the common shares are getting increasingly attractive as well. The consensus estimates call for an EPS of in excess of $2.40 next year, which should backstop the expected dividend increases. If the consensus estimates of a $1.15 annual dividend in 2027 are correct, the dividend yield would increase to 4.4%, even after the recent share price increase.

Given these elements, I think a ‘hybrid’ approach with a combination of common shares and preferred shares could be the best way to deal with Regions Financial.

data.title: Tesla, Alphabet Set To Report Earnings As Investors Watch Out For Jobless Claims And Durable Goods Orders
data.description:
Get ahead of the market by subscribing to Seeking Alpha's Wall Street Week Ahead, a preview of key events scheduled for the coming week. The newsletter keeps you informed of the biggest stories set to make headlines, including upcoming IPOs, investor days, earnings reports, and conference presentations.

Wall Street traded mixed on Friday after the S&P 500 and Nasdaq Composite closed at record highs. According to Deutsche Bank's Jim Reid, the advance was driven by another batch of positive U.S. data, including higher-than-expected retail sales and then a 5th consecutive weekly decline in initial jobless claims. With the stronger data and a continued rise in market inflation pricing, investors dialed down the amount of Fed rate cuts expected this year to 43 bps, the lowest this has been since February. "The problem for the Federal Reserve and markets is that the U.S. administration's policy continues to create considerable uncertainty about the economic outlook," UBS' Paul Donovan said.

Given the uncertainty surrounding the economic outlook, investors should keep a close eye on reports due to be published next week. These include the Richmond Fed Manufacturing Index on Tuesday and MBA Mortgage Applications, Existing Home Sales, Crude Oil Imports, and Gasoline Production on Wednesday. Thursday will include reports on Building Permits, Jobless Claims, and New Home Sales, while Durable Goods Orders and Baker Hughes Rig Count are scheduled to be released on Friday.

Alphabet (GOOGL), Tesla (TSLA), IBM (IBM), Coca-Cola (KO), Philip Morris (PM), Lockheed Martin (LMT), General Motors (GM), Blackstone (BX), Honeywell (HON), Intel (INTC), and Nasdaq (NDAQ) will report earnings next week.

_____________________________________________________________________

Earnings spotlight: Monday, July 21: Verizon (VZ), Domino's Pizza (DPZ). See the full earnings calendar.

Earnings spotlight: Tuesday, July 22: Coca-Cola (KO), Philip Morris (PM), Lockheed Martin (LMT), General Motors (GM). See the full earnings calendar.

Earnings spotlight: Wednesday, July 23: Alphabet (GOOGL), Tesla (TSLA), IBM (IBM), AT&T (T). See the full earnings calendar.

Earnings spotlight: Thursday, July 24: Blackstone (BX), Honeywell (HON), Intel (INTC), Nasdaq (NDAQ). See the full earnings calendar.

Earnings spotlight: Friday, July 25: Phillips 66 (PSX), Centene (CNC). See the full earnings calendar.

Volatility watch: Northern Dynasty Minerals (NAK) and Asset Entities (ASST) are set up for a volatile week of trading based on options volume. The most overbought stocks per their 14-day relative strength index include Monogram Technologies (MGRM), Cogent Biosciences (COGT), and Vigil Neuroscience (VIGL). The most oversold stocks per their 14-day Relative Strength Index include Intapp (INTA) and NextNRG (NXXT).

Dividend watch: Companies that have an ex-dividend next week include Caterpillar (CAT), Paychex (PAYX), Dell Technologies (DELL), CVS Health (CVS), Lowe's Companies (LOW), Levi Strauss (LEVI), Pfizer (PFE), Bank of New York Mellon (BK), and Coca-Cola Consolidated (COKE). Read through some dividend stock picks from Seeking Alpha analysts.

Investor Events: President Trump is scheduled to give a speech on AI on Wednesday, with traders expecting extra volatility for Bitcoin. Wednesday will also see the release of Ciracana's June report on video game hardware and software sales. Meanwhile, the European Central Bank is scheduled to release its monetary policy statement on Thursday. Friday will see the start of the Alzheimer's Association International Conference, which will continue over the weekend in Toronto, Canada. It is the largest international conference dedicated to advancing dementia science. Some of the companies that will present include Acumen Pharmaceuticals (ABOS), Cerevance (CRVN), Marvel Biosciences (MRVL:CA), and Quanterix (QTRX).

Tesla Earnings Preview: Tesla is lined up to report second quarter earnings next week. The electric vehicle maker's report is forecast to show a double-digit decline in both revenue and earnings per share amid slowing EV adoption rates, declining sales in key markets, and some brand backlash against Elon Musk's political interventions. While the reported Q2 numbers may be underwhelming, the update from Elon Musk on the robotaxi rollout, new product timelines, and AI initiatives could be the main story.

Alphabet Earnings Preview: Google owner Alphabet is set to announce its earnings next week. Needham recently raised its earnings estimates and price target on tech giant Google and expects its strategic position and corporate culture to drive valuation upside. According to Needham, Google's current strategic position is strong because of its digital advertising dominance; YouTube, which is the most-streamed service in the U.S.; and GenAI, in which they expect its proprietary LLMs to drive valuation over the next 3-5 years.

Editor's Note: This article covers one or more microcap stocks. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Digital Turbine's Meltdown Completed: Renewed Growth Initiates Seem Promising
data.description:
APPS Remains Attractively Valued Despite The Recent Recovery, Aided By The Growing Monetization Opportunities

We last covered Digital Turbine, Inc. (APPS) in October 2022, discussing why the decreased advertising spending trends had already affected multiple advertising technology and marketing technology companies then, with analysts slashing their forward estimates and, therefore, affecting their prospects/stock prices.

Despite the extreme pessimism then, we did not expect the pessimism to lift in the short term, with the rising inflationary pressure likely to further tighten advertisers' belts and impact the advertising technology companies' profitability, resulting in our reiterated Hold rating then.

APPS 1Y Stock Price

Since then, APPS has indeed retraced by -62.9% compared to the wider market at +67.8%, with the former well underperforming many of its advertising peers to a large degree.

Much of its headwinds are attributed to the slower reversal from the changing ad spending trends since 2022, with it already contributing to the underwhelming 3Y top/bottom-line performance at a CAGR of -13.1%/-40.5% through FY2025, respectively.

Even so, it appears that the worst may very well be behind us, as the APPS stock finally finds a floor by November 2024 and sequentially recovers since then, thanks to the notable improvements observed in its performance metrics.

For example, the ad-tech company recently reported a robust FQ4'25 On Device Solutions revenues of $86.83M (-5.3% QoQ/+10.6% YoY), particularly aided by the growing Revenue Per Device [RPD] of +40% YoY in the US and over +100% YoY internationally.

These numbers build upon the robust RPD growth observed in FQ3'25 across "record levels across both US and international devices," with the softer US device volumes well balanced by the positive revenue growth internationally.

Much of APPS recovery is attributed to its growing global device relationships with numerous OEMs and telecoms, including Motorola (MSI), Nokia (NOK), Microsoft (MSFT), Xiaomi (OTCPK:XIACF), Verizon (VZ), and T-Mobile (TMUS), amongst others.

These naturally allow the ad-tech company to increase the supply of its international RPD while growing the cross-selling of its on-device capabilities, which naturally feed into its in-app advertising growth flywheel.

It is unsurprising then, that the improving performance metrics have contributed to APPS' growing profitability with FQ4'25 adj EBITDA of $20.46M (-7% QoQ/+66.3% YoY) and adj EBITDA margins of 17.1% (+0.8 points QoQ/+6.2 YoY/+8.6 from FY2019 levels of 8.5%).

With market analysts still reporting robust smartphone shipment growth by +2% YoY in Q2'25, led by Samsung (OTCPK:SSNLF) at +8% YoY and MSI at +16% YoY, we believe that the ad-tech company is likely to sustain its outperformance in FQ1'26, if not longer.

The Consensus Forward Estimates

These reasons may also be why the APPS management has felt confident enough to offer a promising FY2026 revenue guidance of $520M at the midpoint (+6% YoY) and adj EBITDA of $87.5M (+21% YoY), with the underwhelming FY2025 performance at $490.5M (-9.9% YoY) and $72.3M (-21.7% YoY) well behind us, respectively.

These metrics have directly contributed to the promising consensus forward estimates as well, with the ad-tech company expected to chart a top/bottom-line growth of +7.5%/+39.2% through FY2027.

When compared to the 5Y historical growth profile at +28.7%/+11.8% and the 10Y growth at +33%/NA, respectively, it is apparent that APPS' efforts to drive renewed growth opportunities through expanded partnerships have been highly successful.

On the other hand, readers may want to closely monitor APPS' debt reliance moving forward, as observed in the growing net debts of -$386.6M in FY2025 (-10.4% YoY/-20,997.3% from FY2019 levels of $1.85M) and the relatively elevated net-debt-to-adj-EBITDA ratio of 5.34x, compared to 3.78x a year ago

Given the minimal free cash flow generation and the impending debt maturity in August 2026, we believe that an expensive debt refinancing is more likely than not, worsened by the rising inflationary pressure by June 2025 and the Fed's hawkish commentary in the recent FOMC meeting.

As a result, we urge investors to monitor APPS' execution for a little longer, especially given the potentially dilutive impact of the expensive interest expenses on its bottom-line performance.

APPS Valuations

For now, the accelerated bottom-line growth profile is why APPS appears to be rather compelling at FWD EV/EBITDA valuations of 10.45x and FWD P/E non-GAAP valuations of 12.53x, compared to the 5Y mean of 23.72x/27.83x, the 5Y pre-pandemic mean of 17.02x/33.26x, and the sector median of 15.25x/24.09x, respectively.

Based on APPS' FWD P/E non-GAAP valuations of 12.53x and the consensus 2Y adj EPS growth estimates of +39.2%, it is apparent that the stock remains cheap at our estimated FWD PEG non-GAAP ratio of 0.31x.

When compared to its profitable competitors, including:

  • Meta (META) at FWD PEG non-GAAP ratio of 1.69x,
  • Snapchat (SNAP) at 0.92x,
  • Unity (U) at 43.1x
  • Magnite (MGNI) at 0.95x,
  • AppLovin (APP) at 0.82x,
  • Alphabet (GOOG) (GOOGL) at 1.31x,
  • Amazon (AMZN) at 1.95x, and
  • The Trade Desk (TTD) at 1.73x,

it is apparent that APPS remains highly attractive here, despite the +329.7% stock price recovery from the November 2024 bottom.

So, Is APPS Stock A Buy, Sell, or Hold?

APPS 7Y Stock Price

For now, APPS' floor has finally materialized at $1.20s ranges by November 2024 after the -98% losses since the February 2021 peak of $88.80s ranges, with the stock seemingly reversing in momentum thanks to the breakthroughs in its performance metrics discussed above.

APPS' Seemingly Durable Upward Momentum

Based on the technical indicators and historical trends above, APPS' upward momentum appears to be durable as well, albeit accompanied by a notable see saw pattern after the last two double-beat performances.

The volatility may also be attributed to the drastically higher short interest volumes by +111.7% on a YoY basis, along with the extreme greed market sentiments by the time of writing.

For now, based on the APPS management's FY2026 adj EBITDA guidance of $87.5M at the midpoint (+21% YoY) and the last shares outstanding of 105.42M, we are looking at an estimated FY2026 adj EBITDA per share of $0.83 (+22% YoY).

Combined with the current FWD EV/EBITDA valuations of 10.45x (discounted compared to the peer group mean of ~23x), it is apparent that the stock is trading at a discount to our fair value estimates of $8.60.

Based on a similar calculation method on the consensus FY2027 adj EBITDA estimates of $91.17M and APPS' resultant FY2027 adj EBITDA per share of $0.86, there remains an excellent upside potential of +71.1% to our long-term price target of $8.90 as well.

A similarly promising result at a long-term price target of $8.50 may also be observed if we use another calculation method based on the FWD P/E non-GAAP valuations of 12.53x (still discounted compared to the peer group mean of ~31x) and the consensus FY2027 adj EPS estimates of $0.68, with it further validating its ongoing reversal.

Combined with the materialization of a trading floor at $1.21 in November 2024, the bullish uptrend support line since then, and the bright long-term prospects through its expanded partnerships, we are rating the APPS stock as a buy.

data.title: Collect 15% From Recession Proof MBS: AGNC
data.description:
Co-authored by Treading Softly

One way that members of High Dividend Opportunities benefit from being income investors is that they reap significant rewards when negative sentiment surrounding a holding persists. Often, negative sentiment is driven by a misunderstanding of the sector itself, and this tends to repeat and propagate. People who repeat such misunderstandings do so with the best of intentions. They believe that they're trying to help you. And in life, we're filled with varied examples where people have misunderstandings, and they tend to share their version until it becomes believed to be a truism, but it's just not correct.

A few examples include the commonly misunderstood belief that we only use 10% of our brain. Another one would be the belief that the usage of a laptop could make you sterile. People would repeat these claims and believe them, even though they were inaccurate, and it was hard to shake people's beliefs, despite them being proven incorrect.

The market is also filled with these.

Today, I want to take a moment and thank every single person who sold shares after my last article. Since that article last month, the performance of AGNC has outperformed the S&P 500 (SP500), which means if you sold me shares, I have outperformed one of the main market indexes. So, thank you so much for doing so; I've been getting value ever since!

Year-to-date, this company has outperformed the general market, and expectations are that it will continue to do so, especially if it's able to raise its dividend—more on that below.

Let's dive in!

Keep On Selling!

AGNC Investment Corp. (NASDAQ:AGNC), yielding 15.5% (and which we've previously covered), is a mortgage REIT that invests in agency MBS. Agency MBS are securities where the mortgages are guaranteed by the agencies—Fannie Mae or Freddie Mac. If a borrower defaults, the agency buys back the mortgage at par. For this reason, agency MBS is viewed as having minimal to no credit risk and trades with a high correlation to US Treasuries.

Agency MBS has a long term, with mortgages having a maturity of 15-30 years, with the 30-year mortgages being by far the most common. However, mortgages are amortizing, which means that with each payment, the borrower is repaying a portion of the principal. Plus, mortgages can be, and frequently are, prepaid. Borrowers refinance, sell their house, or simply prioritize paying down their mortgage early, causing mortgages to often be paid off well before maturity. Additionally, if a borrower defaults, since the mortgage is bought back at par value, economically, that's the same as a prepayment for the mortgage investor. So, while mortgages have a 30-year term on their face, in practice, agency MBS is usually considered to have an expected lifespan of 5-7 years.

AGNC buys agency MBS on a leveraged basis. The leverage that AGNC typically uses is repurchase agreements, or "repos", short-term debt that utilizes agency MBS as collateral. Since agency MBS has minimal credit risk, repo agreements typically have interest rates that are a small spread over the Federal Reserve's target rate.

While we have seen AGNC use leverage of 10x debt/equity in the past, over the past few years, it has kept leverage in the 7.0x to 7.5x range. Source

In plain English, this means that when AGNC buys $100 in MBS, it's borrowing $87.50-$88.25 and funding the rest using cash on hand. This leverage is why we see AGNC's book value move with greater swings than agency MBS prices move.

If we compare AGNC to an ETF that holds agency MBS, we can see that its price is far more volatile, swinging much higher when MBS prices trend high and swinging much lower as MBS prices decline:

When you leverage an asset with a 7-10x debt-to-equity ratio, changes in the asset's value will dramatically impact the value of the equity. AGNC can hedge that impact to some degree, but not entirely. Of course, when you're using 7-10x leverage, you also collect a lot more dividends. MBB is paying 4%, while AGNC is currently paying a 15.5% yield.

Using 7-10x leverage has led to dramatically higher total returns:

Much the same can be said about any leveraged investment. You're experiencing increased volatility in exchange for amplified returns. This is why many investors prefer to use leverage—it offers higher returns.

But using leverage is not without risk. The primary risk of using leverage is that you may have assets with a negative total return. There's a risk of being forced to deleverage, sell assets at unfavorable prices, and make poor decisions due to lender-imposed requirements. You've heard of "margin calls", where lenders require borrowers to pay back early, which usually happens when prices are really bad and the last thing you want to do is sell.

With agency MBS, there is no credit risk. If you hold until maturity, you will get par value back 100% of the time. So, regardless of what happens to MBS prices, if the investor is patient, they will be repaid. But if the investor is using leverage, what happens if the lender demands to be paid back while MBS prices are low?

AGNC protects itself from this risk by maintaining a lot of liquidity. Here's a quote from AGNC's Q1 2025 10-Q discussing this risk:

"Haircut levels and minimum margin requirements imposed by our counterparties reduce the amount of our unencumbered assets and limit the amount we can borrow against our investment securities. During the three months ended March 31, 2025, haircuts on our repo funding arrangements remained stable. As of March 31, 2025, the weighted average haircut on our repurchase agreements was approximately 3.0% of the value of our collateral, compared to 3.2% as of Dec. 31, 2024.

To mitigate the risk of margins calls, we seek to maintain excess liquidity by holding unencumbered liquid assets that can be used to satisfy collateral requirements, collateralize additional borrowings or sold for cash. As of March 31, 2025, our unencumbered assets totaled approximately $6.1 billion, or 64% of tangible equity, consisting of $6.0 billion of cash and unencumbered Agency RMBS and $0.1 billion of unencumbered credit assets."

Note that the average "haircut" is 3%. This means that for MBS valued at $100, AGNC has to provide collateral of $3. So, in theory, they could leverage up at 32x debt/equity before they got a margin call. At 7.5x, AGNC is very far from that possibility.

AGNC carried $6 billion in cash and unencumbered MBS, which could be added as collateral. What happens every day is that the accounts are balanced, and AGNC has to post $X in collateral to the repo lender. Say they start with $97,000 in borrowings, AGNC has to supply $100,000 in collateral because of the 3% haircut.

Now, say MBS prices go down 1%, AGNC is borrowing $97,000, but the collateral is worth only $99,000. AGNC would have to post $1,000 in cash or $1,000 in new MBS to the credit facility.

Say MBS goes up 1%, then AGNC is borrowing $97,000, and the collateral is now worth $101,000. AGNC can remove $1,000 in cash or MBS assets from the collateral pool.

In this fashion, the pool of collateral is updated regularly to match the price changes of agency MBS. AGNC maintains excess liquidity in large part to ensure that it can absorb a shock like March 2020 or the Fed's hiking cycle without being forced to sell MBS.

This allows AGNC to use high leverage on what is a relatively low return. AGNC is holding assets with an average yield of 4.87% and is paying an average cost of funds of 2.75%, meaning that they have a net spread of 2.12%:

Net spread of 2.12% on its own isn't a lot, but when you leverage that spread up 7-7.5x, it becomes very significant.

Over the past two years, AGNC has seen its average yield on assets rise as mortgages have higher coupons. It has also seen its cost of funds rising as short-term rates have gone up and its interest-rate swaps have expired. Looking at the immediate costs, AGNC is receiving ~5.5% on brand-new mortgages and paying ~4.47% on repo borrowings for a spread of 103 bps.

Their spread is likely to remain higher than that because AGNC has the option to enter into new swaps. Remember when we said that MBS has a practical life expectancy of 5-7 years? AGNC only needs to hedge for 5-7 years to be reasonably confident that a large portion of their holdings will repay before the swap expires. The 5 to 7-year interest rate swaps are currently priced around 3.5%: Source

As a result, on new investments, AGNC's spread is around that 200 bps+ range. We can expect variation quarter-to-quarter, but looking forward, the downward trend we have seen in their spread is bottoming around that 200 bps area. This means that earnings in the $0.40's each quarter are sustainable, and AGNC's dividend, coming in at just $0.36/quarter, is underpaying. We've seen peers Dynex (DX) and Anally (NLY) hike their dividends because of this. AGNC has room to raise when it wants to.

Conclusion

AGNC is a classic example of a company that is readily misunderstood, and that misunderstanding is readily repeated in the market. Thankfully, as an income investor, I'm able to collect massive yields and wonderful sums of income over and over again because of this misunderstanding—and so can you. If you feel that AGNC is outside of your risk tolerance, please continue to sell it. I would love for its performance to slow down a bit so I can add more shares to my portfolio and continue to collect wonderful income and market-beating returns. If you, like me, are interested in generating substantial income from the market, then AGNC is a wonderful holding to boost your overall portfolio's income generation. AGNC, with its sky-high yields, falls readily into the category of what I like to call an income catalyst. This means that you won't need extensive exposure to AGNC to boost your income stream significantly.

data.title: I Am Not Convinced The Seas Will Settle For Huntington Ingalls' Shipbuilding Business
data.description:
Investment Thesis

Huntington Ingalls Industries (HII) has suffered margin contractions due to higher production costs from its fixed pre-COVID contracts. Apart from margin concerns, the company is also very dependent on government shipbuilding spending, which has taken a backseat after Trump first announced a new era of shipbuilding in March. While the business should be able to grow in the future, I am concerned about the US Government’s priorities and margin expansion, back to pre-COVID levels; therefore, I am giving HII a Hold rating.

HII Compared To Other Defense Contractors

HII primarily designs and constructs non-nuclear and nuclear ships and submarines for the U.S. Navy and Department of Defense (DoD). While the shipbuilding segment, combining Newport News and Ingalls Shipbuilding, accounted for about 75% of revenue and 80% of operating income in 2024, their Mission Technologies segment accounts for the rest. Their Mission Technologies segment provides national security solutions across cybersecurity, electronic warfare, AI/Machine Learning, and command, control, computers, communications, cyber, intelligence, surveillance, and reconnaissance. It supports all branches of the U.S. military, intelligence agencies, and civilian organizations.

HII contracts are fixed and take several years to complete, leaving them vulnerable to supply chain disruptions, inflationary pressures, and workforce challenges, which can make margins unpredictable. In 2024, 48% of HII's revenue was under fixed-price contracts, where HII bears the risk of cost overruns. In contrast, 47% came from cost-reimbursement contracts, which are more stable as the government can reimburse allowable costs, offering better protection during periods of high inflation or rising raw material costs. Compared to General Dynamics' (GD), 51% fixed price and 43% cost reimbursement; Lockheed Martin's (LMT), 60% fixed price and 40% cost reimbursement, and Raytheon Technologies' (RTX), 54% fixed price and 46% cost reimbursement. Compared to other defense contractors, HII is about average; however, it takes years to build ships compared to aircraft, tanks, or missiles, so their contracts are riskier.

Over the past three years, HII had a 13.7% average gross margin (14.9% 5 year avg), compared to General Dynamics’ 16.1% (16.3% 5yr avg), Lockheed Martin’s 12.2% (12.7% 5yr avg), and Raytheon’s 19.1% (18.8% 5yr avg). While HII’s margin is slightly below average, the variation is greater, mainly due to raw material and inflationary costs from their fixed-priced contracts. Again, due to longer build times, HII is prone to more COGS risk.

HII also has significantly more employees for each dollar of revenue it generates. HII’s 2024 revenue to employee ratio is $262k, compared to General Dynamics’s $434k, Lockheed Martin’s $587k, and Raytheon’s $436k. This high revenue-to-employee ratio is inherent for the shipbuilding business, given the high degree of manual labor, but it again puts more pressure on margins during times of wage inflation and union negotiations.

In my view, these three concerns make HII riskier compared to other companies in the industry, and thus should be priced lower than comparables.

What Has Been Happening With HII Stock Recently

Below is HII's stock price compared to General Dynamics, Lockheed Martin, and Raytheon over the past year.

HII was the most volatile of these defense contractors. HII’s stock dropped 25% in late October due to the company removing its 5-year free cash flow outlook because of future uncertainty, and it dropped again in February after a poor quarter.

HII does not compete with many companies for contracts, apart from General Dynamics, and there are significant economic and technical barriers to entry for the shipbuilding industry. The main question is whether shipbuilding expenditures will continue. Shipbuilding is expensive, and some are questioning whether it is worth the expense, especially with missiles being able to destroy multi-billion-dollar aircraft carriers. Defense planners could decide to spend more on missiles and other technologies rather than warships and submarines. Back in March, Trump said he wanted to revitalize the shipbuilding industry. This comment made HII stock soar over 10%, and the momentum has continued until today, with the stock up over 55% since lows in February. However, there has been no meaningful Congressional backup after his Executive Order. Recently, Ian Bennitt, the senior director for maritime and industrial capacity, left his role for a new one in the private sector. Since his Executive Order in April, 5 of the 7 maritime office workers have departed, so it is unclear if shipbuilding will still be the priority. Especially when the country is more focused on the rising national debt than military programs, like more shipbuilding.

HII's Margins

A constant concern is margins, given the exposure HII has to macrotrends during its multi-year contracts. In 2024, HII reported a 4.6% operating margin compared to a 6.81% margin in 2023. In Q1 2025, HII reported a 5.9% operating margin compared to a 5.5% in Q1 2024. In FY 2025, they expect an operating margin between 5.5% and 6.5%. For reference, General Dynamics’ Marine Systems segment had an operating margin of 6.5% and 7.0% in 2024 and 2023, respectively. In 2025, they expect a 6.8% operating margin for the segment.

These low margins for HII come from pre-COVID contracts, and HII’s costs have gone up since COVID, while revenue has not been reimbursed. Long term, management expects margin expansion from more favorable contracts and greater supply chain control. The operating margin was 8.3%, 11.6%, 11.8%, and 12.4% in 2019, 2018, 2017, and 2016, respectively, for an average of 11.0%. From the current levels, it is reasonable to assume margins will return to pre-COVID levels in the medium term. However, I doubt they will go much higher than 11%, and they could stay low if unexpected inflation or workforce challenges emerge.

DCF Valuation for HII

Management expects $15 billion in revenue by 2030, with growth coming from both the Shipbuilding and Mission Technology segments. This target is ambitious, especially with the uncertainty in shipbuilding, but it is achievable, especially if global tensions heat up. I will assume management will hit this $15 billion target, with revenue growth declining from 2031-2034. I will assume a terminal growth rate of 2.5%, comparable to inflation. Over the last ten years, CAPEX has been 4% of revenue on average, but from 2021-2024, this was only 3.25%. In the future, I would expect about 3.75% of revenue to be used towards capital expenses. I will assume it takes 4 years to get back to normal margins, which I will assume will be 10%, slightly below pre-COVID levels. Margins are the biggest variable. Finally, I am using a 7.3% cost of capital with a 5% cost of debt (0.6% above the risk-free rate) and an 8.34% cost of equity with a 4.4% risk-free rate (10-year US Treasury), a 0.77 unlevered industry beta (0.94 levered), and a 4.2% equity risk premium. My full model is below.

My intrinsic value for HII is $273.6, representing a 7.5% undervaluation at current prices. As stated above, margins are the biggest variable and uncertainty in a growth story like HII. Although lower than pre-COVID, I was quite fair by the rate at which margins would recover and the magnitude of the recovery, 10%. Below, using the same revenue, CAPEX, and WACC assumptions as above, is an intrinsic value table that depends on the final margin and the number of years it takes to achieve.

Years for Recovery/Final Margin

3

4

5

6

7.0%

$164.86

$159.70
